items.rationale: His central thesis: decentralization removes trust dependencies, changes risk profile away from securities, prevents value extraction, and democratizes systems. With the survival fight subsiding, he reframes it as the industry's primary mission to enshrine in law.

He co-designed the DUNA and views legal wrappers as now a strategic imperative after a California ruling exposed DAO members to general-partnership liability. Sees it as the tool to give DAOs legal existence, limited liability, tax pathways, and privacy without compromising decentralization.

Published an extensive token launch playbook; deeply engaged on token economic models, lockups, and insulating tokenholders from bad (illicit) value accrual. Views token design as among the most pressing issues in the industry.

Believes robust app/client layers drive governance minimization and credible neutrality, and that application tokens should coordinate developers (B2B) rather than chase users (B2C), which leads to rent-seeking and speculation.

Sees NFTs as revolutionary digital property rights letting artists engage fans directly and avoid intermediaries. Launched a Creator Defense Fund and frames the SEC's NFT posture as lawfare chilling creator experimentation.
Lists stablecoin payments among the consumer benefits crypto can finally deliver post-election; defends USDC as digital cash and resists KYC creep on holders.
items.age_note: Tempered by concern about winner-take-all stablecoin regulation (see concerns)

items.rationale: Excited by trustless integration of the real-world economy into DeFi; calls onchain government treasuries a massive unlock.
Champions privacy as a fundamental right and zk builders as a net good, while urging risk mitigation so privacy doesn't become synonymous with money laundering.
After the November 2024 election he turns sharply optimistic: opportunity to build on bipartisan progress, bring assets/operations back onshore, and identify/shut down bad actors with clearer rules.
items.age_note: Sharp optimism shift dated to Nov 2024; earlier 2023-24 corpus is dominated by adversarial regulatory posture

items.rationale: Calls security upleveling among the most critical things in crypto; enthusiastic about MetaLeX trust-minimized governance tooling for projects pursuing decentralization.
Calls decentralized computing breakthroughs the future and urges the US to keep the technology onshore.
items.age_note: Single statement April 2024, not restated; cited but aging

items.rationale: His sharpest recurring concern: speculative, non-productive tokens with no underlying value enrich insiders, harm retail, and set the industry back on both tech and regulatory fronts. Sees casino-culture advocates as talking their book.

Persistently frames the SEC's enforcement-first, rulemaking-avoidant approach as politically motivated lawfare that fosters uncertainty, targets the most compliant actors, and stretches Howey toward ordinary commerce.
items.age_note: By late 2024 he sees this lawfare beginning to unwind under the incoming administration

items.rationale: Argues fast paths to liquidity (like SPACs) breed vaporware and retail harm; pushes 4-year/1-year lockups and decentralization-milestone unlocks so insiders can't dump before tech delivers.
Repeatedly warns founders against fixed token allocations and short lockups, which misalign incentives, hand investors hold-up rights, and signal intent to dump.
Flags coordinated debanking of pre-revenue, pre-token portfolio companies as unjustified; predicts a denial-to-acceptance progression and treats it as a live abuse needing exposure.

Views the IRS's expanded broker rule as a fantastical reading of 'effectuate transactions' designed to ban DeFi, contradicting Uniswap/Coinbase rulings; confident it won't survive but treats it as a serious threat.
Beyond crypto, deeply alarmed by openly weaponized law (SEC, NY DA, CA speech laws, Chevron-era agency overreach); sees a 'doom loop' that raises election stakes and corrodes rule of law.

A California ruling means mere DAO participation (even forum posts) could trigger general-partnership liability for other members' actions — a threat he says can hold DAOs hostage and a key reason to adopt the DUNA.
Argues the offshore foundation model is grift-laden gatekeeping that should be replaced by onshore structures like the DUNA now that the US is becoming buildable.

While acknowledging legitimate reasons, he opposes a single-asset reserve or crypto-specific tax breaks as government handouts that distort a level playing field; prioritizes neutral rules over subsidies.
Concerned that concentrating power in a single stablecoin issuer (or a surveillance-state CBDC) undermines decentralization across web3.
Worries that absent the right incentives, economic gravity drives projects to remain centralized or fake decentralization, producing web2-style value extraction (Facebook/Apple/Amazon chains).

His go-to proof that sufficient decentralization moves a token outside securities laws; cites the ETH ETF/futures approvals as the SEC formally conceding ETH isn't a security, establishing a path for all projects. Also his model of a token with real value that isn't equity-like.

Treats Bitcoin as the archetypal decentralized commodity that Howey cannot reach — used as a legal benchmark for what a non-security token looks like. (Note: he's enthusiastic about BTC as an asset class, but opposes a government BTC reserve.)
items.age_note: Excitement is conceptual/legal, not a price call; paired with opposition to a strategic BTC reserve

items.rationale: Sees the bulk of memecoins as get-rich-quick vehicles with no underlying value (no protocol, art, or games) that harm retail and the industry's credibility; warns mis-designed value accrual rules could push everything toward memecoins.

Cites UST/Anchor as a clear securities offering (issuer-operated, non-decentralized, unsustainable yield) and a cautionary template; warns CeFi yield products and yield-bearing stablecoins should take note.
items.age_note: Stated Dec 2023, not restated in 2024; relevant but aging

risk_regime.rationale: His most recent (Nov-Dec 2024) posture is structurally constructive: post-election he sees the existential regulatory fight subsiding, the US becoming one of the best places to build, lawfare beginning to unwind, and the agenda shifting to building/decentralization in 2025. This optimism is firmly conditioned, not unconditional — he remains acutely wary of the speculative/casino segment, fast-liquidity dumping, debanking, and lingering IRS/agency overreach. His 'risk-on' is about long-term building and US onshoring, not endorsement of speculative trading.

notes: Miles Jennings is a16z crypto's General Counsel/head of decentralization, so his 'market view' is policy-, legal-, and design-centric rather than price/trading-oriented; he rarely names individual tokens as trades. Dominant through-line across the entire 18-month corpus is decentralization (defined late-2024 as absence of control) as both the legal limiting principle for Howey and crypto's core purpose. Clearest recency-driven shift: a hostile/defensive regulatory tone through mid-2024 flips to forward-looking optimism after the Nov 2024 election, while specific late-2024 threats (debanking, IRS DeFi broker rule, CA DAO-liability ruling) sharpen. Consistent secondary themes: long lockups over short, B2B over B2C tokens, onshore DUNA over foreign foundations, and opposition to government handouts/single-asset reserves in favor of a level playing field. ETH and BTC appear mainly as legal exemplars of non-security decentralized assets rather than investment picks.
